summary: Martin Fowler uses the 2012 US election and Nate Silver's probabilistic forecasting as a lens to examine widespread probabilistic illiteracy. He argues that people fundamentally misunderstand probability - conflating an 85% chance with certainty, believing single events can validate probabilistic models, and demanding binary answers rather than ranges. Fowler connects this to software development, where stakeholders want firm estimates rather than probability distributions. He contends that as data becomes more central to decision-making, software professionals must both understand statistics themselves and educate consumers of their data to distinguish signal from noise.
key_points: An 85% probability is not a prediction of certainty - it means there's still a meaningful 15% chance of the alternative outcome, like rolling a 6 on a die
Single events cannot validate or invalidate probabilistic models; you would need many repetitions to test the underlying distribution
People's demand for certainty over probabilistic ranges leads to serious errors in project planning and decision-making
The difference between those who embrace their biases versus those who strive for objectivity is meaningful, even if absolute objectivity is impossible
Software developers have a responsibility to help users understand probability and statistics as data becomes more central to our lives
money_quotes: Silver is saying his model forecasts an 85% chance of an Obama victory, which is not at all the same thing as predicting an Obama victory.
One event cannot say much about an underlying distribution. You'd have to hold many tens of elections to really test the model.
I've developed a strong distrust of certainty, to the point that the more certain someone seems to be, the less I'm inclined to believe them.
People want to hear a binary answer rather than a probabilistic one. We see this, of course, in project planning where people want firm numbers rather than ranges.
That difference between 85% and 100% can lead to some serious errors.
While absolute objectivity is impossible, if you make the effort it is possible to get closer to objectivity than just wallowing in your prejudices.
An important part of this is helping people to see the difference between signal and noise.
key_insight: Understanding probability is essential for making sense of data, and software professionals must lead in both developing this literacy themselves and educating others to distinguish signal from noise.
